skill.name: vite
skill.description: Use Vite reference guidance to configure dev servers, plugins, environment modes, builds, SSR, libraries, and performance options.
skill.summary: Configure, build, and optimize Vite projects with focused framework reference guidance.
skill.author: antfu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and environment alerts, but reviewed locations are markdown documentation examples for Vite. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or executable skill code was found; residual risk is low because the references discuss environment files and development server settings.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger Static Command Alerts
low_findings.description: The command alerts are shell snippets and inline code inside Vite reference markdown, not executable skill logic. The reviewed examples document normal Vite CLI usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations are fenced markdown code blocks showing Vite commands. No executable script or command invocation is present in the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Environment Guidance Needs Careful Use
low_findings.description: The references explain Vite environment loading and include examples for .env files. This is legitimate documentation, but users must avoid exposing secrets in client bundles.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content includes explicit safety notes about VITE-prefixed variables and envPrefix. Risk is limited to user misuse of documented configuration patterns.

low_findings.title: Filesystem And Dynamic Import Alerts Are Documentation
low_findings.description: The filesystem and dynamic import findings are Vite SSR and glob import examples. They describe framework APIs and do not add hidden file access to the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed lines are explanatory markdown examples for Vite behavior. They are not executed by the skill and do not show exfiltration intent.

content.value_statement: Vite projects often need precise configuration across dev, build, SSR, and plugins. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused Vite reference guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains Vite config files, defineConfig, modes, aliases, and environment loading.
Guides common CLI workflows for dev servers, production builds, and preview commands.
Covers plugin usage, plugin ordering, virtual modules, and hook behavior.
Supports build topics including SSR, library mode, assets, CSS, and multi-page apps.
Provides performance guidance for dependency pre-bundling, caching, and build optimization.
limitations: It is a reference skill, not a live Vite project analyzer.
It does not install packages or run builds by itself.
It is based on Vite 6.x documentation generated on 2026-01-28.
Framework-specific behavior still depends on each project and plugin version.

use_cases.title: Set Up A New Vite App
use_cases.description: Choose config patterns, scripts, aliases, and env modes for a clean project start.

use_cases.title: Prepare Production Builds
use_cases.description: Tune assets, targets, minification, chunking, manifests, and preview workflows before release.

use_cases.title: Extend Vite With Plugins
use_cases.description: Understand plugin hooks, ordering, virtual modules, and integration points for custom tooling.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Config
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a basic Vite configuration for my app. Include scripts, aliases, and safe environment variable guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Dev Server Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Vite dev server needs and recommend settings for host, port, CORS, HMR, and dependency optimization.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Production Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Vite production build plan for assets, CSS splitting, sourcemaps, targets, manifests, and preview validation.

prompt_templates.title: Design Advanced Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Vite SSR or plugin integration. Explain hooks, server behavior, build outputs, and security considerations.

output_examples.input: I need a simple Vite setup for a TypeScript app.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan covering config structure, package scripts, TypeScript support, aliases, and safe VITE-prefixed environment variables.

output_examples.input: My production build needs better asset handling.
output_examples.output: A build checklist covering public assets, inline limits, output directories, CSS splitting, sourcemaps, and preview testing.

output_examples.input: I want to write a custom Vite plugin.
output_examples.output: A plugin design outline explaining lifecycle hooks, ordering, virtual modules, transform behavior, and when to use existing plugins.

best_practices: Keep secrets out of VITE-prefixed variables because they can reach client bundles.
Use official plugin patterns before adding custom build or transform logic.
Validate production output with vite preview before deploying build artifacts.
anti_patterns: Do not expose all environment variables through an empty env prefix.
Do not add custom plugins when a maintained Vite plugin already solves the task.
Do not treat development server options as production server hardening.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps configure Vite projects, plugins, development servers, production builds, SSR, assets, CSS, and environment modes.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it run Vite commands for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference guidance. The host assistant or user decides whether to run commands.

faq.question: Which Vite version is covered?
faq.answer: The skill states that it is based on Vite 6.x and was generated on 2026-01-28.

faq.question: Does it cover environment variables?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains VITE-prefixed variables, modes, .env files, loadEnv, and security notes for secrets.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation false positives and no malicious runtime behavior.
